subsection: Estimating the Among-group Variance
nearby_text: These expressions for $ V_B $ overestimate the true among-line variance $ \sigma_B^2 $, as the sample means are measured with error. In particular, $ \overline{z}_i = \mu_i + e_i $, so that $$ \sigma^{2}(\overline{z}_{i})=\sigma^{2}(\mu_{i})+\sigma^{2}(e_{i})=\sigma_{B}^{2}+\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}}{n} $$ (12.8d) where $ \sigma_z^2 $ is the trait variance and $ n $ is the sample sized used to estimate $ \mu_i $. label: Formula 12.8e
subsection: Estimating the Among-group Variance
nearby_text: a number of authors (Lynch 1988a, 1990; Turelli et al. 1988; Bjöklund 1991; Savalli 1993), a simple way to avoid this issue is to estimate the among-group variance from a standard one-way ANOVA, with $$ V_{B}(t)=\frac{MS_{B}-MS_{W}}{n_{0}} $$ (12.8e) using the among- and within-group mean squares $ (MS_{B} $ and $ MS_{W} $, respectively), with $ n_{0} $ being a measure of the average sample size per group (see LW Table 18.1).

label: Formula 12.9c
subsection: Lande's Constant Variance Test, $ F_{CV} $
nearby_text: If we ignore the (usually) small founder effect, Equation 12.1b returns $$ \sigma_{B}^{2}(t)\simeq2f_{t}\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)=2\left[1-\left(1-\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\right)^{t}\right]\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)\simeq t\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)/N_{e}\quad\mathrm{f o r}t\ll N_{e} $$ (12.9c) and hence $$ V_{B}(t)\sim\left(\frac{t\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)}{N_{e}[L-1]}\right)\chi_{L-1}^{2} $$ (12.9d)

label: Formula 12.13
section: The Neutral Divergence of Quantitative Traits: Introduction
subsection: LONG-TERM DIVERGENCE
nearby_text: For $ \tau < 1 $, the temporal approach to the equilibrium level of divergence is defined by the mutation rate $ (u) $, assuming an identical $ N_e $ in the base and descendant populations, $$ \sigma_{B}^{2}(t)=\left[1-(1-u)^{2t}\right]\widetilde{\sigma}_{B}^{2} $$ (12.13) and hence is quite slow (approximately 2u per generation).

label: Formula 12.14b
section: TESTING THE NULL HYPOTHESIS OF NEUTRAL PHENOTYPIC DIVERGENCE: RATE-BASED TESTS

label: Formula 12.14c
section: TESTING THE NULL HYPOTHESIS OF NEUTRAL PHENOTYPIC DIVERGENCE: RATE-BASED TESTS
subsection: Lande's Brownian-motion Model of Neutral Trait Evolution
nearby_text: For example, assuming a constant effective population size, the additive genetic variance at time t under drift and mutation is given by Equation 11.20b. Substituting this into Equation 12.14b yields $$ \begin{align*}\sigma_{t}^{2}&=\frac{1}{N_{e}}\sum_{i=0}^{t-1}\left[2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}+\left(\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)-2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}\right)e^{-i/2N_{e}}\right]\\&=2\sigma_{m}^{2}t+\left(\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)-2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{N_{e}}\sum_{0=1}^{t-1}e^{-i/2N_{e}}\right)\end{align*} $$ (12.14c)

label: Formula 12.16a
section: TESTING THE NULL HYPOTHESIS OF NEUTRAL PHENOTYPIC DIVERGENCE: RATE-BASED TESTS
subsection: Lande's Brownian-motion Model of Neutral Trait Evolution
nearby_text: 88). Lande assumed a constant variance, $ \sigma_t^2 = t\sigma_A^2/N_e $, but because part of his concern was evolution in the fossil record, he replaced $ \sigma_A^2 $ by $ h^2\sigma_z^2 $, yielding $$ \sigma_{t}^{2}=h^{2}\sigma_{z}^{2}t/N_{e} $$ (12.16a)

label: Formula 12.18a
section: TESTING THE NULL HYPOTHESIS OF NEUTRAL PHENOTYPIC DIVERGENCE: RATE-BASED TESTS
subsection: Tests Based on the Brownian-motion Model
nearby_text: \sigma_z^2 / N_e$. Recalling that $\Pr(|U| \leq 1.96) = 0.95$, Lande’s critical effective population size below which there is a $< 5%$ probability of an absolute deviation as large as $d$ satisfies $$ 1.96=\frac{d}{\sqrt{th^{2}\sigma_{z}^{2}/N_{e}}},\quad implying\quad(1.96)^{2}th^{2}\sigma_{z}^{2}=N_{e}d^{2} $$ (12.18a)

label: Formula 12.21a
section: The Neutral Divergence of Quantitative Traits: Introduction
subsection: Tests Based on the Brownian-motion Model
nearby_text: Thus, the hypothesis of drift is rejected (at $ \alpha = 0.05 $) if the mutational heritability is too small to account for the observed divergence rate, namely $$ h_{m}^{2}<0.10\cdot\Delta\simeq0.10\cdot\frac{d_{*}^{2}}{t} $$ (12.21a)

label: Formula 12.21b
section: The Neutral Divergence of Quantitative Traits: Introduction

nearby_text: Conversely, the divergence is too slow to be accounted for by drift if the assumed mutational heritability is too high to account for the observed divergence rate, or when $$ h_{m}^{2}\geq509\cdot\Delta\simeq509\cdot\frac{d_{*}^{2}}{t} $$ (12.21b)

label: Formula 12.21c
section: The Neutral Divergence of Quantitative Traits: Introduction
subsection: Tests Based on the Brownian-motion Model
nearby_text: ght recall Equation 12.8c, where we showed that $ V_B = d^2/2 $. So why did we assume that $ \Delta \simeq d_*/t $ in these two equations? Recalling that $ \sigma_e^2 = (1 - h^2)\sigma_z^2 $, we have $$ \Delta=\frac{V_{B}}{t\sigma_{e}^{2}}=\frac{d^{2}}{2t(1-h^{2})\sigma_{z}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2(1-h^{2})}\frac{d_{*}^{2}}{t}\simeq\frac{d_{*}^{2}}{t} $$ (12.21c) with the last step following when $ h^2 \simeq 1/2 $.

  "nearby_text": "ght recall Equation 12.8c, where we showed that $ V_B = d^2/2 $. So why did we assume that $ \\Delta \\simeq d_*/t $ in these two equations? Recalling that $ \\sigma_e^2 = (1 - h^2)\\sigma_z^2 $, we have $$ \\Delta=\\frac{V_{B}}{t\\sigma_{e}^{2}}=\\frac{d^{2}}{2t(1-h^{2})\\sigma_{z}^{2}}=\\frac{1}{2(1-h^{2})}\\frac{d_{*}^{2}}{t}\\simeq\\frac{d_{*}^{2}}{t} $$ (12.21c) with the last step following when $ h^2 \\simeq 1/2 $."
}

For large $ t $, the mean value approaches the optimal value $ (\\theta) $, while the divergence variance approaches an asymptotic val"
}

label: Formula 12.23a
subsection: Tests for Departures From Symmetric Random Walks
nearby_text: symmetric random walk is $ \sigma\sqrt{n} $, where $ \sigma^2 $ is the variance in change per increment. Assuming roughly equal time intervals between samples, this standard error can be estimated as $$ \widehat{\sigma^{2}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i}^{n}(x_{i}-x_{i-1})^{2} $$ (12.23a)

  "nearby_text": "symmetric random walk is $ \\sigma\\sqrt{n} $, where $ \\sigma^2 $ is the variance in change per increment. Assuming roughly equal time intervals between samples, this standard error can be estimated as $$ \\widehat{\\sigma^{2}}=\\frac{1}{n}\\sum_{i}^{n}(x_{i}-x_{i-1})^{2} $$ (12.23a)"
}

label: Formula 12.23b
subsection: Tests for Departures From Symmetric Random Walks
nearby_text: the first $ k $ increments (the displacement of the mean from its original value after $ k $ steps). Bookstein obtained a large- $ n $ expression for the distribution of the largest scaled excursion, $$ \gamma=\frac{\max_{k}\left|S_{k}\right|}{\widehat{\sigma}\sqrt{n}} $$ (12.23b)

label: Formula 12.25a
subsection: Hunt's Approach for Comparing Different Models
nearby_text: e penalizing for the number of parameters (k) with smaller AIC values implying a better model. With $ n < 40k $ observations, Anderson et al. suggested that a corrected version of AIC should be used. $$ \begin{align*}AIC_c=-2\ln(L)+2k+{2k(k+1)\over n-k-1}\end{align*} $$ (12.25a) which differs from the standard AIC measure in the addition of the last term. label: Formula 12.25c
subsection: Hunt's Approach for Comparing Different Models
nearby_text: The resulting Akaike weights for each of the candidate models are given by $$ \begin{align*}w_i=\frac{e^{-(\Delta_i/2)}}{\sum_{j=1}^m e^{-(\Delta_i/2)}}\end{align*} $$ (12.25c)

subsection: Hunt's Approach for Comparing Different Models
nearby_text: odel equal prior weight (for m models, a prior weight of 1/m on each cancels in the denominator and numerator of Equation 12.25c). More generally, if one has prior weights $ \pi_{i} $ for each model, $$ w_{i}=\frac{\pi_{i}e^{-(\Delta_{i}/2)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m}\pi_{j}e^{-(\Delta_{i}/2)}} $$ (12.25d)

label: Formula 12.26a
subsection: $ Q_{ST} $: Partitioning Additive Variance Over Populations
nearby_text: s $ \sigma_{GW}^2 = (1 - Q_{ST})\sigma_G^2 $ and $ \sigma_{GB}^2 = 2Q_{ST}\sigma_G^2 $, respectively, for a total variance in a structured population of $ (1 + Q_{ST})\sigma_G^2 $. Rearranging yields $$ Q_{ST}=\frac{\sigma_{GB}^{2}}{\sigma_{GB}^{2}+2\sigma_{GW}^{2}} $$ (12.26a)

Rearranging yields $$ Q_{ST}=\\frac{\\sigma_{GB}^{2}}{\\sigma_{GB}^{2}+2\\sigma_{GW}^{2}} $$ (12.26a)"

label: Formula 12.26b
subsection: $ Q_{ST} $: Partitioning Additive Variance Over Populations
nearby_text: et al. 2010), $ \sigma_{GW}^2 $ in Equation 12.26a is weighted by one, rather than two. More generally, when f is the amount of inbreeding within each population, then following Bonnin et al. (1996), $$ Q_{ST}=\frac{(1+f)\sigma_{GB}^{2}}{(1+f)\sigma_{GB}^{2}+2\sigma_{GW}^{2}} $$ (12.26b)

label: Formula 12.27a
subsection: $ P_{ST} $: Approximating $ Q_{ST} $ with Phenotypic Data
nearby_text: a standard ANOVA (e.g., Holand et al. 2011), while the latter require a series of parent-offspring or sib rearings in a common environment. A modification of this purely phenotypic approach is to use $$ \widehat{\sigma}_{G B}^{2}=c\widehat{\sigma}_{P B}^{2},\qquad\widehat{\sigma}_{G W}^{2}=h^{2}\widehat{\sigma}_{P W}^{2} $$ (12.27a) where c reflects the fact that only part of an observed phenotypic difference in means may be genetic (Merilä 1997; Leinonen et al. 2006; Sæther et al. 2007; Brommer 2011). label: Formula 12.27b
subsection: $ P_{ST} $: Approximating $ Q_{ST} $ with Phenotypic Data
nearby_text: c difference in means may be genetic (Merilä 1997; Leinonen et al. 2006; Sæther et al. 2007; Brommer 2011). label: Formula 12.28a
subsection: Testing $ Q_{ST} $ Versus $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: itative trait is equivalent to that from a single-marker $ F_{ST} $. Provided that the average $ F_{ST} $ is small, then under the null that $ Q_{ST} = F_{ST} $, to a very good approximation, we have $$ \frac{n_{d}-1}{\overline{F}_{ST}}Q_{ST}\sim\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1},\quad\mathrm{implying}\quad Q_{ST}\sim\frac{\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1} $$ (12.28a) where $ \overline{F}_{ST} $ is the average $ F_{ST} $ over the scored molecular marker loci, and $ n_{d} $ is the number of demes. This expression assumes that $ Q_{ST} $ is estimated without error,

  "nearby_text": "itative trait is equivalent to that from a single-marker $ F_{ST} $. Provided that the average $ F_{ST} $ is small, then under the null that $ Q_{ST} = F_{ST} $, to a very good approximation, we have $$ \\frac{n_{d}-1}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}Q_{ST}\\sim\\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1},\\quad\\mathrm{implying}\\quad Q_{ST}\\sim\\frac{\\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1} $$ (12.28a) where $ \\overline{F}_{ST} $ is the average $ F_{ST} $ over the scored molecular marker loci, and $ n_{d} $ is the number of demes. This expression assumes that $ Q_{ST} $ is estimated without error,"
}

label: Formula 12.28b
subsection: Testing $ Q_{ST} $ Versus $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: $ Q_{ST} $ is restricted to $ (0, 1) $. Hence, the approximation given by Equation 12.28a assumes that there is essentially no probability in the upper tail of a $ \chi^{2} $ above a critical value, $$ \Pr\left(\frac{\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>1\right)=\Pr\left(\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>\frac{n_{d}-1}{\overline{F}_{ST}}\right)\simeq0 $$ (12.28b)

Hence, the approximation given by Equation 12.28a assumes that there is essentially no probability in the upper tail of a $ \\chi^{2} $ above a critical value, $$ \\Pr\\left(\\frac{\\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>1\\right)=\\Pr\\left(\\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>\\frac{n_{d}-1}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}\\right)\\simeq0 $$ (12.28b)"

label: Formula 12.28c
subsection: Testing $ Q_{ST} $ Versus $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: Insight into power is obtained by asking, under the null, how often the ratio $ Q_{ST}/F_{ST} $ exceeds some value, $ \delta $. Rearranging Equation 12.28a yields $$ \Pr\left(\frac{Q_{ST}}{\overline{F}_{ST}}>\delta\right)=\Pr\left(\frac{(n_{d}-1)Q_{ST}}{\overline{F}_{ST}}>\delta(n_{d}-1)\right)=\Pr\left(\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>\delta(n_{d}-1)\right) $$ (12.28c)

        "id": "formula_12.28a",
        "latex": "\\frac{n_{d}-1}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}Q_{ST}\\sim\\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1},\\quad\\mathrm{implying}\\quad Q_{ST}\\sim\\frac{\\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1}",
        "label": "Formula 12.28a",
        "section": "TIME SERIES DIVERGENCE TESTS",
        "subsection": "Testing $ Q_{ST} $ Versus $ F_{ST} $",
        "position": 73,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "n"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_12.28b",
        "latex": "\\Pr\\left(\\frac{\\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>1\\right)=\\Pr\\left(\\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>\\frac{n_{d}-1}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}\\right)\\simeq0",
        "label": "Formula 12.28b",
        "section": "TIME SERIES DIVERGENCE TESTS",
        "subsection": "Testing $ Q_{ST} $ Versus $ F_{ST} $",
        "position": 74,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "S",

          "\\Pr",

          "\\overline{F}",

  "nearby_text": "Insight into power is obtained by asking, under the null, how often the ratio $ Q_{ST}/F_{ST} $ exceeds some value, $ \\delta $. Rearranging Equation 12.28a yields $$ \\Pr\\left(\\frac{Q_{ST}}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}>\\delta\\right)=\\Pr\\left(\\frac{(n_{d}-1)Q_{ST}}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}>\\delta(n_{d}-1)\\right)=\\Pr\\left(\\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>\\delta(n_{d}-1)\\right) $$ (12.28c)"
}

label: Formula 12.29
subsection: Testing $ Q_{ST} $ Versus $ F_{ST} $
nearby_text: $ \widehat{Q}_{ST} $ value, Whitlock and Guillaume (2009) started with the clever observation that because $ Q_{ST} = F_{ST} $ for a neutral additive trait, Equation 12.26a can be rearranged to yield $$ \sigma_{GB}^{2}=\frac{2F_{ST}\sigma_{GW}^{2}}{1-F_{ST}} $$ (12.29)

subsection: Closing Comments: $ Q_{ST}, F_{STQ} $, and Linkage Disequilibrium
nearby_text: Kremer (2003) quantified the influence of LD on $ Q_{ST} $ by noting that the relationship between $ Q_{ST} $ (based on variance components) and $ F_{STQ} $ (based on the underlying loci) is given by $$ Q_{ST}=\frac{(1+\phi_{a})F_{STQ}}{(\phi_{a}-\phi_{w})F_{STQ}+1+\phi_{w}} $$ (12.30c) where $ \phi_x $ is given by Equation 12.30a. nearby_text: ves a p value of $ 1/2 \sim 0.05 $, while for n = $ n_{high} = 5 $, the smallest p is $ 1/16 \sim 0.0625 $. For large values of n, Orr noted that Equation 12.31a can be approximated by a normal, with $$ \Pr\left(n_{+}\geq n_{high}\mid n_{+}\geq[n/2]\right)\simeq2\left[1-\Phi\left(\frac{n_{high}-[n/2]}{\sqrt{n/4}}\right)\right] $$ (12.31b) where $ \Phi(x) = \Pr(U \leq x) $ for $ U \sim N(0,1) $.

latex: p=\Pr(n_{+}\geq n_{high}\mid G\geq D)=\sum_{i\geq n_{high}}^{n}\Pr(n_{+}=i\mid G\geq D)
label: Formula 12.32
section: TRAIT-AUGMENTED MARKER-BASED APPROACHES: TESTS USING QTL INFORMATION
subsection: Orr's QTLST and QTLST-EE Sign Tests
nearby_text: The resulting distribution of plus alleles in the high line is now conditioned on neutrality (signs drawn at random), the assumed distribution of QTL effects, and the actual divergence $D$, yielding $$ p=\Pr(n_{+}\geq n_{high}\mid G\geq D)=\sum_{i\geq n_{high}}^{n}\Pr(n_{+}=i\mid G\geq D) $$ (12.32) where $ \Pr(n_{+}=i|G\geq D) $ is simply the fraction of times that exactly $ i $ plus alleles were found in the retained bootstrap samples (i.e., those showing a divergence of at least $ D $). Alter

nearby_text: arker changes the trait by a value of $ g_i $, with $ p_{i,j} $ denoting the frequency of this allele in population j. The GWAS-predicted mean genetic value for the trait in population j thus becomes $$ a_{j}=2\sum_{i=1}^{m}g_{i}p_{ij} $$ (12.33b)

        "latex": "\\frac{n_{d}-1}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}Q_{ST}\\sim\\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1},\\quad\\mathrm{implying}\\quad Q_{ST}\\sim\\frac{\\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\\chi^{2}_{n_{d}-1}",

      {
        "id": "formula_12.28b",
        "latex": "\\Pr\\left(\\frac{\\overline{F}_{ST}}{n_{d}-1}\\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>1\\right)=\\Pr\\left(\\chi_{n_{d}-1}^{2}>\\frac{n_{d}-1}{\\overline{F}_{ST}}\\right)\\simeq0",

        "id": "formula_12.31a",
        "latex": "\\Pr(n_{+}\\geq n_{high}\\mid n_{+}\\geq[n/2])=\\frac{\\Pr(n_{+}\\geq n_{high})}{\\Pr(n_{+}\\geq[n/2])}=\\sum_{i\\geq n_{high}}^{n}\\binom{n}{i}\\bigg/\\sum_{j\\geq[n/2]}^{n}\\binom{n}{j}",

        "label": "Formula 12.31b",

  "nearby_text": "arker changes the trait by a value of $ g_i $, with $ p_{i,j} $ denoting the frequency of this allele in population j. The GWAS-predicted mean genetic value for the trait in population j thus becomes $$ a_{j}=2\\sum_{i=1}^{m}g_{i}p_{ij} $$ (12.33b)"

label: Formula 12.33h
section: TRAIT-AUGMENTED MARKER-BASED APPROACHES: TESTS USING GWAS INFORMATION
subsection: The Berg and Coop $ Q_{x} $ Test
nearby_text: This is the basis for the Berg-Coop $ Q_{x} $ test, whose statistic is given by $$ Q_{x}=\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{x}=\frac{(\mathbf{a}^{*})^{T}\boldsymbol{\Omega}^{-1}\mathbf{a}^{*}}{2V_{A}} $$ (12.33h)

label: Formula 12.34a
subsection: Rate-based Tests for Neutrality in Divergence of Gene Expression
nearby_text: e used to estimate the among-group variance and $ k $ individuals per line were used to estimate $ V_A $, Equation 12.5a shows that both estimators approximately follow $ \chi^2 $ distributions, with $$ V_{B}\sim\left(2t\sigma_{m}^{2}\right)\cdot\chi_{L-1}^{2}/\left(L-1\right)\qquad and\qquad V_{A}\sim\left(2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}\right)\cdot\chi_{k-1}^{2}/\left(k-1\right) $$ (12.34a) where t is the time of divergence since the common ancestor. These expressions suggest a modified version of Lande's $ F_{MDE} $ test statistic, $$ F_{M D E}^{*}=\frac{V_{B}/(2t\sigma_{m}^{2})}{V_{A}

These expressions suggest a modified version of Lande's $ F_{MDE} $ test statistic, $$ F_{M D E}^{*}=\\frac{V_{B}/(2t\\sigma_{m}^{2})}{V_{A}/(2N_{e}\\sigma_{m}^{2})}=\\frac{V_{B}}{V_{A}}\\cdot\\left(\\frac{N_{e}}{t}\\right)\\sim\\frac{\\chi_{L-1}^{2}/(L-1)}{\\chi_{k-1}^{2}/(k-1)} $$ (12.34b) where this statistic follows an $F$ distribution, with $F_{MDE}^* \\sim F_{L-1,k-1}$ (as it is the ratio of two $\\chi^2$ random variables, scaled by their degrees of freedom; see LW Appendix 5). A sca"

label: Formula 12.34c
subsection: Rate-based Tests for Neutrality in Divergence of Gene Expression
nearby_text: and hence suggestive of stabilizing selection, while a scaled ratio in excess of $F_{1-\alpha/2}$ implies too much divergence, suggestive of directional selection. These critical values are given by $$ \frac{V_{B}}{V_{A}}\leq F_{\alpha/2,L-1,k-1}\left(\frac{t}{N_{e}}\right)\qquad and\qquad\frac{V_{B}}{V_{A}}\geq F_{1-\alpha/2,L-1,k-1}\left(\frac{t}{N_{e}}\right) $$ (12.34c) where $ F_{\alpha,M,N} $ denotes critical values for an F distribution and satisfies $$ \Pr\left(F_{M,N}\leq F_{\alpha,M,N}\right)=\alpha $$

nearby_text: e case where just two populations (L = 2) are compared by using their squared difference, $ d^2 $, then recalling that $ V_B = d^2/2 $ (Equation 12.8c), the conditions given by Equation 12.34c become $$ \frac{d^{2}}{V_{A}}\leq F_{\alpha/2,1,k-1}\left(\frac{2t}{N_{e}}\right)\qquad\mathrm{o r}\qquad\frac{d^{2}}{V_{A}}\geq F_{1-\alpha/2,1,k-1}\left(\frac{2t}{N_{e}}\right) $$ (12.34d)
